coredata.dc:title: A novel fluorescent sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in corn flour
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Jianmei
Zhou, Yuting
Li, Zhijuan
Wang, Ting
Sun, Qi
Le, Tao
Jirimutu,
coredata.dc:description: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) contamination in cereals and their products seriously concerns food safety and human health. Herein, a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-based fluorescent sensing platform was successfully developed for the sensitive and rapid detection of AFB1 in corn flour. In this work, using sodium citrate and urea as precursors, hydrophilic N-CQDs were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method with good biocompatibility and high fluorescence stability. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence in the 300–370 nm range, and had bright blue emission at 448 nm upon 360 nm excitation. Via fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs in the presence of increasing AFB1 concentrations, the proposed fluorescent sensor had an excellent selectivity for AFB1 detection with a low limit of detection (0.13 ng/mL) in the linear range of 0.5–100 ng/mL. Under optimized sensing conditions, the rapid AFB1 determination in real corn flour samples was realized with good recoveries (95.3–105.7%). In summary, present results indicated the promising potential of this novel N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor for rapid and effective AFB1 detection in agricultural products.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescent sensor
Aflatoxin B1
Nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots
Rapid testing
Corn flour

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of AFB1 in real corn flour samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2023 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) observation; (B) Size distribution and (C) FT-IR curves of as-synthesized nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs).

(A) UV–Vis absorption, (B) PL spectra, and (C) the maximum fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of N-CQDs. (D) N-CQDs in aqueous solution under sunlight (left) and 365 nm UV light (right) irradiation. The PL intensity of N-CQDs under different (E) storage times in daylight and (F) NaCl concentrations.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra and (B) relative fluorescent intensity of N-CQDs with different mycotoxins at the same concentration. Effects of (C) solution pH and (D) incubation time on the relative fluorescent intensity changes of N-CQDs after AFB1 addition. Graph bars with different letters on top represent statistically significant results (p < 0.05) based on one-way ANOVA analysis.

(A) Linear calibration curve of the normalized fluorescent intensity versus different AFB1 concentrations (0.5–100 ng/mL). Inset: Fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CQDs at various AFB1 concentrations under excitation at 360 nm. (B) Selectivity evaluation regarding the fluorescence response of N-CQDs to different interferents. (C) Stability test of the proposed fluorescent sensor during 10 days.
Schematic diagram of aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) detection by hydrothermal-assisted synthesis of N-CQDs through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism.
Comparison of the constructed sensor with other methods for AFB1 detection.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Note: MB-methylene blue; GCE-glassy carbon electrode; MWCNTs-multiwall carbon nanotubes; N-CQDs-nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots; NR: not reported.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detection of AFB1 spiked in negative corn flour samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A novel fluorescent sensing platform based on nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots for rapid and sensitive detection of aflatoxin B1 in corn flour

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) contamination in cereals and their products seriously concerns food safety and human health. Herein, a nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs)-based fluorescent sensing platform was successfully developed for the sensitive and rapid detection of AFB1 in corn flour. In this work, using sodium citrate and urea as precursors, hydrophilic N-CQDs were synthesized by the one-step hydrothermal method with good biocompatibility and high fluorescence stability. The as-prepared N-CQDs exhibited excitation wavelength-independent fluorescence in the 300–370 nm range, and had bright blue emission at 448 nm upon 360 nm excitation. Via fluorescence quenching of the N-CQDs in the presence of increasing AFB1 concentrations, the proposed fluorescent sensor had an excellent selectivity for AFB1 detection with a low limit of detection (0.13 ng/mL) in the linear range of 0.5–100 ng/mL. Under optimized sensing conditions, the rapid AFB1 determination in real corn flour samples was realized with good recoveries (95.3–105.7%). In summary, present results indicated the promising potential of this novel N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor for rapid and effective AFB1 detection in agricultural products.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Aflatoxins are a class of heterocyclic secondary metabolites with low molecular weights (286–346 Da) produced mainly by Aspergillus, Fusarium, Penicillium and other fungi (Kim & Lee, 2021). The sensitive and efficient detection of aflatoxins is essential to ensure food safety and human health due to their carcinogenic, teratogenic, and mutagenic properties (Benkerroum, 2020). Aflatoxin B1 (AFB1) has been recognized as a Class I carcinogen, and more than 100 countries have legislated its maximum levels in agricultural and food products (Rushing & Selim, 2019). Given its low threshold and high toxicity, quantitative AFB1 determination in food and feed has raised health and economic concerns.

Some analytical techniques are available for testing AFB1 in food and feed matrices. Chromatography methods such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) and liquid chromatography-mass spectroscopy (LC-MS) are accurate and reliable (Dhanshetty et al., 2022; Wang, Pan, et al., 2019). However, these methods can be tedious, time-consuming, and expensive, often requiring specialized equipment and personnel to obtain a confident quantification. In contrast, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and other immunoassay techniques are simpler than chromatography (Ren et al., 2019; Zhao, Jin, et al., 2021). While ELISA-based AFB1 tests are usually fast and cheap, they still have significant limitations, such as long incubation times, low sensitivity, and false-positive results.

Many new testing methods have been developed to address the limitations of traditional methods. Antibodies-based electrochemical immunosensors have been established to detect AFB1 based on different nanomaterials (Wei et al., 2022). Furthermore, researchers have developed a carbon quantum dots/octahedral Cu2O nanocomposite-based electrochemical aptamer sensor for detecting AFB1 in wheat flour with a broad linear range (Rahimi et al., 2021). Despite their high specificity and sensitivity, these antibodies or aptamers-based electrochemical methods have notable limitations, including high preparation costs and operational complexity. Additionally, these electrochemical sensors generally require complex electrode pretreatment and rigorous immobilization. Therefore, highly efficient, sensitive, and rapid methods must be developed for AFB1 detection in real samples.

As one of the promising and reliable alternatives, fluorescent biosensors have gained increasing attention due to their simplicity, low cost, convenient instrumentation, and high sensitivity (Qiao et al., 2021) (Zhao et al., 2021). As a new class of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with sizes below 10 nm, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have been extensively used as an ideal luminescent probe for bioimaging (Al-Hetty et al., 2023), photothermal therapy (Zhao et al., 2023), and optoelectronics (Salihoglu et al., 2018). Compared to traditional organic fluorescent dyes, CQDs have excellent properties such as superior photoluminescence and photostability, good biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, inexpensive cost, and ease of preparation and functionalization (Cheng et al., 2023). As a novel luminescent nanomaterial, CQDs have been widely applied to detect organic pollutants, food additives, and antibiotics (Kaur et al., 2023; Liu, Du, et al., 2023). For example, Cai et al. (2023) prepared novel CQDs with red emission at 595 nm, and the fluorescence of as-obtained CQDs could be specifically quenched by malachite green. Based on this, a fluorescent sensing platform was developed to rapidly detect malachite green in fish tissue within 1 s with a low detection limit (12.9 nmol/L). Diverse heteroatoms, including metallic and non-metallic atoms, were doped into CQDs to further improve its fluorescence performance for sensitive detection of trace contaminants (Liu et al., 2019; 2023). However, metal atom doping can easily cause ineffective and inhomogeneous doping, and biological toxicity. In contrast, non-metallic atomic doping is a good option for fluorescence sensing detection of contaminants. Recently, Liu, Du, et al. (2023) reported a fluorescence method based on nitrogen doped CQDs for quantitatively detecting naphthenic acids in wastewater. Nevertheless, no studies have reported the interaction between CQDs and mycotoxins. Meanwhile, the aforementioned good properties give CQDs the prospect of possible applications for rapid and sensitive detection of mycotoxins.

Herein, a facile hydrothermal route was applied to rapidly prepare water-soluble nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) using sodium citrate and urea as carbon and nitrogen sources, and their chemical structure and optical properties were characterized. The resulting N-CQDs can be used as fluorescent probes to create a convenient, cost-effective fluorescent sensor for sensitive and rapid AFB1 detection in corn flour. Therefore, this work opens a new avenue for AFB1 detection and demonstrates the great potential of N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensors in practical applications.

Sodium citrate and urea were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Patulin (PAT), ochratoxin A (OTA), zearalenone (ZEN), aflatoxin B1 (AFB1), and deoxynivalenol (DON) were obtained from Shanghai Yuanye Bio-technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Corn flour samples were purchased from local supermarkets in Chongqing, China. All other chemicals were analytical grade and deionized Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ/cm) was used throughout the experiments.

Briefly, 1.970 g of sodium citrate and 2.413 g of urea were ground into a white powder and poured into a 50 mL Teflon-lined autoclave. The hydrothermal reactants were heated at 180 °C for 2 h and dispersed in 30 mL of deionized water (Scheme 1 ). Insoluble impurities were removed by centrifugation (10, 000 rpm, 10 min). The resulting upper solution was dialyzed for 24 h using 100 Da cutoff dialysis bags, then lyophilized using a freeze dryer. Finally, N-CQDs were diluted in PBS buffer solution to the desired concentration before use.

A comprehensive characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs was performed using different techniques. Morphological and dimensional information was acquired by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The surface structure and functional groups were detected using Fourier transmission infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, EQUINOX 55, Germany). The ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectrum of N-CQDs was determined by a UH5300 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) in the 200–600 nm range. The fluorescence spectra were tested using an F-7100 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature.

The quenching effect of different mycotoxins, including AFB1, OTA, DON, ZEN and PAT, on the fluorescence of N-CQDs was first investigated to fabricate highly selective fluorescent sensors. The mixed solution containing 100 μg/mL of N-CQDs and 0.5 ng/mL of different mycotoxins was incubated for 4 min at room temperature. Afterward, the effect of different mycotoxins on the fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs was determined. The fluorescence quenching efficiency was evaluated as F0/F, where F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of N-CQDs in the absence and presence of mycotoxins, respectively.

The fluorescent detection of AFB1 in the standard solution was carried out as follows: AFB1 was mixed at the final concentrations of 0–100 nM with 100 μg/mL of N-CQDs and incubated for the optimal time at room temperature. Subsequently, the mixed solution was adjusted to the optimal pH and the final volume of 250 μL was kept fixed throughout the experiments. The effects of different AFB1 concentrations on the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs were recorded, and their fluorescence responses were measured based on the normalized fluorescence intensity values (ΔF=F0–F/F0). The limit of detection (LOD) for fluorescent AFB1 sensing was estimated using the 3σ/s rule (Lee et al., 2019), where σ is the standard deviation of 11 blanks, and s is the slope of the plotted calibration curve. Under the same assay conditions, diverse interferents were detected at five times AFB1 concentration of for the anti-interference analysis of the fabricated fluorescent sensor.

The feasibility of the developed N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor was tested with 1 g (accurate to 0.01 g) of negative corn flour confirmed by HPLC. For spiking experiments, AFB1 was first added at 2.5, 5 and 50 ng/mL. Then, AFB1 was extracted from spiked samples by adding 2 mL of 70% (v/v) methanol to each sample and placing it on a shaker (180 rpm) for 30 min at room temperature (Han et al., 2022). The resulting supernatant was collected by centrifugation at 9, 286 g for 10 min and diluted four times with PBS buffer solution. AFB1 levels in spiked samples were measured using the assay described in this work and HPLC. Each sample was analyzed in triplicate.

Experiments were performed at least in triplicate, and data are expressed as means ± standard deviation. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) with post hoc Tukey's method was used in this study. Differences between the means were determined as statistically significant (p < 0.05) using SPSS 22.0 (SPSS Inc., USA).

The observation of transmission electron microscopy (TEM) revealed that as-synthesized N-CQDs were well dispersed in water and the individual particles exhibited a highly spherical structure with a uniform size of 5.5 nm (Fig. 1 A and B). The high-resolution TEM image further indicated the formation of tiny N-CQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm (Fig. 1A).

The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1C) showed broad peaks at 3421-3230 cm−1, corresponding to O–H and N–H bond stretching vibrations. Additionally, a sharp peak at 1592 cm−1 was identified as the CC stretching vibration of the carboxylic acid, while the characteristic peak at 1466 cm−1 was attributed to the C–NH vibration. Finally, the broad peak observed at 500-1000 cm−1 was assigned to C–O stretching vibrations. The presence of these functional groups improves the aqueous solution stability and enhances the hydrophilicity of CQDs (Tang et al., 2021). Besides, the nitrogen in N-CQDs can form active sites, leading to an increase in sp2 cluster fraction and an enhancement of surface roughness and enhancing their hydrophilicity and dispersibility (Wang, Pan, et al., 2019).

The optical characterizations of as-prepared N-CQDs were further analyzed using UV–Vis and fluorescence spectroscopy. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs presented two peaks-a broad and weak peak at 260 nm and a prominent peak at 330 nm (Fig. 2 A). The peak corresponding to the π-π* transition obtained at 260 nm was due to aromatic CC bonds, while the other peak at 330 nm represents the n-π* transition of the CO functional group in N-CQDs (Luo et al., 2017).

Additionally, the fluorescence emission peak position of N-CQDs was not affected by different excitation wavelengths, but the fluorescence intensity was significantly affected (Fig. 2B). The optimal excitation and emission wavelength of as-prepared N-CQDs were located at 360 and 448 nm, respectively (Fig. 2C). Accordingly, the aqueous solution of N-CQDs appears brownish-yellow under sunlight and turns blue under 365 nm UV light irradiation (Fig. 2D). Moreover, the N-CQDs presented strong radiation resistance as evidenced by a slight attenuation of the fluorescence intensity after 15 days of sunlight exposure (Fig. 2E). This resistance might be attributed to the stable chemical structure and surface functional groups of N-CQDs, including hydroxyl, carbonyl, and amino groups (Zhang et al., 2019). Moreover, high concentrations of NaCl had a negligible effect on fluorescence intensity, even when the concentration of NaCl reached 1 mmol/L (Fig. 2F). These results demonstrated that as-prepared N-CQDs have excellent photo-stability for subsequent fluorescent detection.

Based on fluorescence responses of N-CQDs to different mycotoxins, including DON, PAT, ZEN and OTA, the selectivity and feasibility of the designed fluorescent sensing system to AFB1 was evaluated. Significantly reduced fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs solution was detected after AFB1 introduction (Fig. 3 A), and the F0/F fluorescence value (F0 and F stand for the fluorescence intensity values of N-CQDs without and with mycotoxins, respectively) was the highest among all mycotoxins tested (Fig. 3B). However, the effect of other mycotoxins on the fluorescence intensity was negligible compared to the blank N-CQDs solution. Hence, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs can be prominently quenched by AFB1 compared to other mycotoxins. These results might be driven by the ligands of CQDs, which would affect its quenching selectivity toward mycotoxins. As a π-electron-rich carbon material, N-CQDs can interact with AFB1 which also has abundant π-electrons through π-π stacking interactions. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs can be quenched by AFB1 when the two molecules are close up to a certain distance. Furthermore, the collision between the fluorescent donor and acceptor might cause the excited state of N-CQDs to lose its excitation energy and return to the ground state via energy or charge transfer mechanisms (Molaei, 2020). The UV–Vis absorption spectrum indicated that AFB1 has a maximum absorption peak at 365 nm and a wide range of 300–550 nm. Additionally, this wavelength range can overlap well with the emission spectrum of N-CQDs, demonstrating the potential for fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) between the two entities. As FRET is a dynamic quenching process, fluorescence quenching and recovery should be accompanied by a change in fluorescence lifetime (Zhao, Jin, et al., 2021). When a high AFB1 concentration (50 ng/mL) was added, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CQDs was significantly reduced from 7.29 ns to 5.56 ns, which provided further evidence for the existence of FRET between N-CQDs and AFB1. Therefore, the underlying principle for AFB1 detection using the N-CQDs fluorescent probe was mainly due to FRET dynamic quenching process in which N-CQDs and AFB1 can be considered as energy donor-acceptor pairs (Scheme 1).

Moreover, some related parameters in fluorescent detection including reaction time and pH, were optimized to achieve the best analytical performance. The incubation time of the added AFB1 with N-CQDs solution is important for the fluorescent sensing process. The fluorescence values (F0/F) significantly increased with increasing reaction times within 1–4 min, then gradually decreased from 4 to 10 min (Fig. 3C). Therefore, 4 min was chosen as the optimal incubation time for detecting AFB1 using the N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor. The fluorescence intensity changes of N-CQDs affected by AFB1 were evaluated in the pH range of 5–9. Increasing the pH from 5 to 7 gradually increased the fluorescence quenching value (F0/F), reaching the highest value at pH 7 and subsequently decreasing from pH 7 to 9 (Fig. 3D). This changing trend might be ascribed to the disturbance of ionization equilibrium and alternations in the molecular structure of AFB1 under different pH environments (Liu, Cao, et al., 2019). The coumarin moiety of AFB1 can be altered under alkaline conditions (Anjum et al., 2022), and considering that AFB1 is unstable under alkaline conditions and the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs is strongest under neutral conditions, the pH of 7 was selected as the optimum pH condition in subsequent analysis. Besides, N-CQDs showed good potential for food sample detection since most food products have a pH value between 5 and 7.

The sensing performance of N-CQDs based fluorescent sensor was studied by analyzing different AFB1 concentrations under the above optimal conditions. These results indicated that the emission peaks of N-CQDs at 448 nm gradually declined with decreasing AFB1 concentrations (the inset of Fig. 4 A). The plot of the normalized fluorescence values of N-CQDs at 448 nm versus AFB1 concentration in PBS solution (pH = 7.0) is shown in Fig. 4A. A good linear relationship existed between the ΔF values of N-CQDs and the AFB1 in the concentration range of 0.5–100 ng/mL. The linear equation was ΔF = 0.3707 lgCAFB1+0.2179 (R2 = 0.9911). Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) for the developed fluorescent sensor was estimated as 0.13 ng/mL with 11 times the noise in the blank as the accepted standard.

The sensitivity, rapidity, and simplicity of the fluorescent sensor based on N-CQDs were also compared with other reported detection methods for AFB1 (Table 1 ). The sensor's sensitivity was comparable to other methods, except for photoelectrochemical and electrochemiluminescent methods (Liu, Cao, et al., 2019; Tian et al., 2023) which required time-consuming and complicated steps. Therefore, the N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor constructed in this work exhibited the advantages of simple preparation, convenient analysis, time-saving, satisfactory sensitivity, and wide linear range. This study demonstrated the effectiveness of a stable and cost-efficient N-CQDs fluorescent probe for AFB1 detection with high selectivity and sensitivity. Therefore, this sensor might be a suitable tool for monitoring AFB1 levels in practice.

Moreover, the anti-interference capabilities were evaluated in the presence of various interfering agents to verify the selectivity of the fluorescent sensing system. Interfering mycotoxins did not affect the fluorescence signals of the sensing system even at 5-fold concentrations of AFB1 (Fig. 4B). Hence, the fabricated N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor has good specificity for AFB1 detection. Besides, the fluorescent sensor exhibited good stability with a relative standard deviation (RSD) range of 1.97–3.75% (Fig. 4C), making it highly suitable for practical applications. These results can be attributed to the remarkable storage stability and aqueous dispersion of the N-CQDs used in this work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of AFB1 in real corn flour samples
ce:section.ce:para: The mean recovery rates of corn flour samples under three different spiked concentrations of AFB1 for the present fluorescent sensor were 95.3–105.7% with a coefficient of variation (CV) of 1.71–9.43% (Table 2 ). The detection results of the N-CQDs-based sensor displayed a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.9979) with those obtained by HPLC. Additionally, the RSD values of five AFB1 measurements were less than 9.1%. The above results indicated that the proposed fluorescent sensor based on N-CQDs exhibited good repeat ability, and it can be used for rapid AFB1 detection in real agricultural products.

In summary, a novel, facile, and economical fluorescent sensor was successfully constructed based on the fluorescence resonance energy transfer principle between N-CQDs and AFB1, which can be applied for sensitive and rapid detection of AFB1 in real corn flour. The as-prepared N-CQDs synthesized by the hydrothermal method exhibited a sphere-like structure and displayed excitation-independent fluorescent behavior with bright blue fluorescence emission. The obtained N-CQDs also had good water solubility, excellent fluorescence stability, high selectivity and fluorescence response to AFB1. Under optimized conditions, the N-CQDs-based fluorescent sensor showed a good linear range (0.5–100 ng/mL) and a low detection limit (0.13 ng/mL). The proposed fluorescent sensor also had excellent specific selectivity and repeat-ability and can rapidly detect AFB1 in corn flour samples with satisfactory recoveries (95.3–105.7%). Therefore, this study demonstrated the effectiveness of a stable and cost-efficient N-CQDs fluorescent probe in detecting AFB1 with high selectivity and sensitivity.

Jianmei Li: Investigation, Software, Writing – original draft. Yuting Zhou: Investigation, Data curation, Software. Zhijuan Li: Visualization, Software. Ting Wang: Validation. Qi Sun: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Tao Le: Supervision, Project administration. Jirimutu: Funding acquisition, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was financially supported by grants from the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2020YFE0203300) and the Science and Technology Research Program of Chongqing Municipal Education Commission (KJQN202100516).
